title: Topological Fukaya categories with coefficients
speaker: Tobias Dyckerhoff
abstract: Abstract: Within an emerging approach to Fukaya categories via cohomology with categorical coefficients, I will outline a theory of a particularly nice class of nonconstant coefficient systems defined on Riemann surfaces. These are categorical analogues of perverse sheaves, called perverse schobers. We provide a definition of perverse schobers as categorical sheaves on a relative two-colored version of the unital Ran space of the surface.

proof_stages.label: Classical perverse sheaves on a stratified Riemann surface
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the Kontsevich-inspired program in context and recalls perverse sheaves on a Riemann surface with finitely many special points as objects of the constructible derived category satisfying shifted-local-system and stalk-boundedness conditions, equivalently the heart of a glued t-structure stable under Verdier duality.

proof_stages.label: Local linear model and spherical-adjunction target
proof_stages.summary: The standard local classification on the complex plane with one singular point is recalled in terms of nearby and vanishing cycle vector spaces with canonical and variation maps, and its formal analogy with spherical adjunctions motivates defining perverse schobers as stable-infinity-categorical perverse sheaves locally modeled by spherical functors.

proof_stages.label: Relative-cohomology reformulation by basic pairs
proof_stages.summary: To avoid categorifying derived constructible sheaves directly, the speaker introduces relative cohomology for basic open pairs, uses purity for perverse sheaves to obtain degree-zero vector spaces, and states a full-faithful presheaf embedding whose essential image is characterized by normalization, homotopy invariance, and descent.

proof_stages.label: Paracyclic local analysis and Dold-Kan recovery
proof_stages.summary: In the local one-singularity case, restricting to basic pairs whose support contains the special point and localizing weak equivalences produces the paracyclic category; Dold-Kan and its paracyclic upgrade recover the two-term nearby/vanishing-cycle diagram with monodromy, while self-duality of the paracyclic category foreshadows Verdier duality.

proof_stages.label: Definition of perverse schobers
proof_stages.summary: The presheaf characterization is categorified by replacing vector spaces with stable infinity categories and imposing normalization, homotopy invariance, strip descent, co-descent, and a recollement condition expressing admissible semiorthogonal decomposition data.

proof_stages.label: Spherical S-construction for local classification
proof_stages.summary: The technical local construction uses the relative Waldhausen S-construction and an in-progress spherical upgrade to turn spherical adjunctions into paracyclic diagrams of stable infinity categories satisfying the schober axioms, with the expected local equivalence not yet fully proved.

proof_stages.label: Global paracyclic surface category and gluing formalism
proof_stages.summary: The localization of the basic-pair poset admits a combinatorial paracyclic category of the surface that is self-dual, supports a Verdier-duality-like interchange of sheaves and cosheaves, satisfies a van Kampen theorem for local gluing, and allows Kan-extension/chiral-homology-style global sections.

proof_stages.label: Fukaya-Lefschetz application and derived-section problem
proof_stages.summary: For an exact Lefschetz fibration, vanishing cycles give spherical functors that glue to a perverse schober whose supported sections recover Fukaya-Seidel, regular-fiber, and total-space Fukaya categories, while the resulting merely left-exact sequence suggests the need for a derived secondary global-section theory.

examples.name: No singular points gives a local system
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that when the special set is empty the schober/perverse-sheaf analogue is just a local system, making the singular-point data concrete without affecting the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: When asked about the case of empty singular set, the speaker only states that it is just a local system.

examples.name: Basic pair from Re(z^(n+1))
examples.note: The pair of regions defined by inequalities on the real part of z^(n+1) illustrates what a basic pair looks like and how relative cohomology measures change across a critical value.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives the pair defined by inequalities involving Re(z^(n+1)) as an example of a basic pair and says what it measures, but does not compute or verify the example in detail.

passes.reason: The Re(z^(n+1)) model is a standard regular n+1-pronged basic pair with rotational symmetry.
the pair defined by level sets of Re(z^(n+1)) is a regular cyclic model of a basic pair rather than an adversarial example

The Re(z^(n+1)) construction is a regular cyclic local model for a basic pair, used illustratively.

the Re(z^(n+1)) model is a regular dihedrally symmetric family of basic pairs, used as a positive normal example
the pair cut out by Re(z^(n+1)) is a regular canonical family with cyclic rotational symmetry, used illustratively rather than adversarially

examples.name: Two basic pairs with non-basic intersection
examples.note: The sketched intersection of two basic open pairs shows why the basic-pair poset is not closed under intersections and why ordinary sheaf-topological arguments need replacements.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses a drawn pair of basic opens and examines their intersection to show concretely that the intersection need not be basic.

passes.reason: The two basic pairs are used to show that intersections of basic pairs need not be basic.

examples.name: Exact Lefschetz fibration and Fukaya categories
examples.note: Applying the schober formalism to Seidel's exact Lefschetz fibrations both illustrates the construction from vanishing-cycle spherical functors and generates the final idea that global sections should have a derived secondary version.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the schober construction to an exact Lefschetz fibration, traces regular fibers, vanishing paths/cycles, spherical functors, gluing, and identifies resulting Fukaya-category sections.

passes.reason: The exact Lefschetz fibration is used as a typical application producing Fukaya-category data via a schober.
